title: Accumulate SSE data lines before JSON decode (tool calling + streaming)
preview: Fixes JSONDecodeError in Python streaming chat when tool_call payloads contain embedded newlines and are emitted as multiline SSE `data:` entries. Prior code attempted JSON.parse per line. This PR accumulates all `data:` lines for an event, joins per SSE spec, then decodes once. Adds fixtures for tool_call frames and ensures backward compatibility with single-line events.
text: Fixes JSONDecodeError in Python streaming chat when tool_call payloads contain embedded newlines and are emitted as multiline SSE `data:` entries. Prior code attempted JSON.parse per line. This PR accumulates all `data:` lines for an event, joins per SSE spec, then decodes once. Adds fixtures for tool_call frames and ensures backward compatibility with single-line events.

title: introduce-hedged-retry-hints-for-streaming-clients-crosslang-ergonomic-surface-and-version-bump
preview:
Motivation: production inference workloads occasionally suffer long-tail token latencies (cold microbursts, GC pauses, noisy co-tenants). This PR introduces a small, opt-in hedged-retry mechanism and a lightweight stream hint that lets SDKs coordinate a second speculative request when a short latency threshold is exceeded. The goal is to reduce 99.9th percentile token latency for streaming without changing the server-side contract or requiring model changes.

What this PR does (single continuous description): Adds a cross-SDK ergonomic surface for hedged retries and stream hints: Python: StreamingClient.stream(..., hedge_policy=HedgePolicy(...)) TypeScript: streamingClient.stream({ ..., hedgePolicy }) Go: Stream(ctx, opts.WithHedgePolicy(...)) The hedge policy is intentionally configuration-light: backoff_before_hedge, max_hedges, concurrency_penalty_hint. SDKs attach a small opaque hint header (x-rw-stream-hint) so routers/edge can make better scheduling/fallthrough decisions. The implementation keeps token refresh / credential rotation behavior unchanged and integrates with existing retry presets.

Included work: - API surface in each SDK (py/ts/go) with typed docs + examples - Lightweight in-process hedger that duplicates only minimal request metadata for the speculative call; KV cache is reused where possible - New retry presets: hedge-optimistic, hedge-conservative - Integration tests: end-to-end with local serving fixture that simulates 2x latency spikes - Unit tests for credential interplay and cancellation semantics - CI matrix updated to run crosslang hedged e2e on a slow-path fixture - Version bumps: python v3.14.0 -> v3.15.0, typescript v1.28.0 -> v1.29.0, go v1.8.3 -> v1.9.0

Backward compatibility and safety: The hedged path is opt-in and disabled by default in all SDKs. When enabled, the SDK ensures only one response stream is surfaced to consumers: the first to produce tokens. The speculative request is canceled upon token emission and its telemetry is recorded as a hedged_sample. Credentials and session heartbeats are not duplicated; the hedged request reuses the same session token when possible to avoid extra auth roundtrips.

Notes on ergonomics: The API favors simple presets (hedge='optimistic'|'conservative'|'off') and exposes a HedgePolicy object for advanced workloads. We keep streaming iterator/callback shapes identical; hedged behavior is transparent to consumer code unless they opt into hedged metrics.

Security and audit: No new privileges are required. Hedged requests are logged with the same level of audit metadata and sanitized in telemetry.

Related work: ENG-8123 (hedged-retry design), ENG-8199 (router hints), SUP-1402 (customer report of long-tail streaming latency).

Checklist: - [x] API design across py/ts/go - [x] Unit tests for hedger and cancel semantics - [x] E2E slow-path fixture tests - [x] Telemetry hooks and dashboard notes added - [x] Docs and changelog entries - [x] Version bumps and package publish configs updated

Motivation: production inference workloads occasionally suffer long-tail token latencies (cold microbursts, GC pauses, noisy co-tenants). This PR introduces a small, opt-in hedged-retry mechanism and a lightweight stream hint that lets SDKs coordinate a second speculative request when a short latency threshold is exceeded. The goal is to reduce 99.9th percentile token latency for streaming without changing the server-side contract or requiring model changes.

What this PR does (single continuous description): Adds a cross-SDK ergonomic surface for hedged retries and stream hints: Python: StreamingClient.stream(..., hedge_policy=HedgePolicy(...)) TypeScript: streamingClient.stream({ ..., hedgePolicy }) Go: Stream(ctx, opts.WithHedgePolicy(...)) The hedge policy is intentionally configuration-light: backoff_before_hedge, max_hedges, concurrency_penalty_hint. SDKs attach a small opaque hint header (x-rw-stream-hint) so routers/edge can make better scheduling/fallthrough decisions. The implementation keeps token refresh / credential rotation behavior unchanged and integrates with existing retry presets.

Included work: - API surface in each SDK (py/ts/go) with typed docs + examples - Lightweight in-process hedger that duplicates only minimal request metadata for the speculative call; KV cache is reused where possible - New retry presets: hedge-optimistic, hedge-conservative - Integration tests: end-to-end with local serving fixture that simulates 2x latency spikes - Unit tests for credential interplay and cancellation semantics - CI matrix updated to run crosslang hedged e2e on a slow-path fixture - Version bumps: python v3.14.0 -> v3.15.0, typescript v1.28.0 -> v1.29.0, go v1.8.3 -> v1.9.0

Backward compatibility and safety: The hedged path is opt-in and disabled by default in all SDKs. When enabled, the SDK ensures only one response stream is surfaced to consumers: the first to produce tokens. The speculative request is canceled upon token emission and its telemetry is recorded as a hedged_sample. Checklist: - [x] API design across py/ts/go - [x] Unit tests for hedger and cancel semantics - [x] E2E slow-path fixture tests - [x] Telemetry hooks and dashboard notes added - [x] Docs and changelog entries - [x] Version bumps and package publish configs updated

title: credential-stager-and-async-stream-helpers-python-sdk
preview: Context: Several teams reported friction when integrating token rotation and bursty streaming workloads. This PR introduces a CredentialStager utility (lightweight prefetch + lease queue) and an async_stream helpers module to make backpressure-friendly async iterables easier to compose across long-lived streaming sessions. Motivation: reduce auth-related failed requests during token handoff and improve ergonomic composition for consumer code (less boilerplate around cancellations, reconnection hints, and graceful drains). Design goals: (1) keep public surface minimal and opt-in, (2) reuse existing credential interfaces so cross-SDK parity is achievable, (3) add telemetry hooks for future rollout. What this PR does: - Add redwood_sdk/credentials/stager.py (CredentialStager): prefetches refresh tokens, exposes acquire/lease/release semantics, supports pluggable backoff policies. - Add redwood_sdk/streams/async_helpers.py: provides async_partitioned_iter, drain_with_timeout, and a small stream combinator (zip_async) that preserves cancellation semantics. - Wire CredentialStager into streaming client internals as an optional parameter (client ctor kwarg: credential_stager). - Add docstrings, examples, and migration notes in docs/streaming.md and examples/streaming/credential-stager-example.py. - Tests: unit tests for stager lease behavior, integration test that simulates token expiry during an ongoing stream, and a small e2e that exercises reconnection with pre-warmed credentials. Related: coordinated with TS/Go SDK maintainers to align API shapes (see ENG-1292). Rollout: default behavior unchanged; customers must opt-in to enable stager via new kwarg. Migration: guide included to show how to pass existing credential providers into the stager. Checklist: - [x] Implementation - [x] Unit tests - [x] Integration tests - [x] Docs and example - [x] CI green - [x] Cross-SDK API proposal posted

title: Add offline econ prober and quant preset importer to SDK (local-sim tooling)
preview: Context: teams frequently iterate on prefill/batching/quant profiles but lack a lightweight way to run unit-econ simulations locally before pushing changes to the platform. This PR adds an offline prober and quant preset importer to the Python SDK so devs can: 1) run fast local simulations against sampled workloads, 2) import/validate quantization YAMLs produced by the model tooling, and 3) generate a compact cost delta report to guide conservative rollout decisions. Motivation: reduce deploy-retry cycles, catch regressions in cost/latency tradeoffs earlier, and make onboarding for non-infra engineers simpler. What changed: added redwood.econ.offline.Prober and redwood.econ.importer. The prober accepts sample sets (JSONL/ndjson) and runs a simulated batching/cache pipeline with configurable SLO and token distributions; it reports token-level unit-econ, cold/cached latency deltas, and a risk score for aggressive quant settings. The importer validates quant preset YAMLs (structure, supported ops, target bitwidths) and emits a normalized preset that matches the platform schema (but does not auto-deploy). Safety: telemetry is opt-in; by default runs in dry-run mode and warns when presets would cause >5% estimated perplexity delta (requires an eval anchor). Backwards compatibility: zero-impact on existing APIs unless users explicitly import and call the new classes. Included docstrings, a short quickstart in README, and a test harness with small synthetic samples. Checklist: - [x] CLI bindings: redwood econ probe/import (shim to SDK) - [x] Importer validation + normalization - [x] Local simulation engine with simple batching model - [x] Unit tests for importer and prober - [x] README quickstart and example sample files - [x] Security review (no network calls by default) - [x] End-to-end smoke run on CI Design notes: the simulator models batching using a configurable window/timeout heuristic and supports per-request token-length distributions. The risk score combines estimated unit-econ delta, latency tail amplification, and a simple quality proxy (if eval anchors provided). Limitations: this is a lightweight estimator and is not a replacement for full tenant-sim runs in the control plane. Migration: none required; consumers can opt into the new API. Example usage: 1) redwood econ probe samples.ndjson --preset local_qs.yaml --slo 50ms 2) from redwood.econ import offline; Prober(...).run("samples.ndjson", preset="local_qs.yaml") Testing & results: synthetic smoke across 3 sample sets shows estimated token cost changes within 8% of platform sim for small models (reconciled by downstream tenant-sim). Shipping notes: add to SDK changelog under "Tooling", callout in release announcement for Platform/SEs.

title: ergonomic-session-proxy-and-adaptive-retry-presets-py-ts-go
preview: Motivation: ergonomics around session lifecycle and retry policies across our three SDKs remains a frequent source of customer confusion. This PR introduces a small, language-idiomatic SessionProxy abstraction plus a set of adaptive retry presets that provide sane defaults for streaming and request/response flows. The goal is parity of behavior across Python/TypeScript/Go without forcing a single API shape. Summary of changes: 1) Add SessionProxy (py/ts/go) - a thin ergonomic wrapper over the existing session/credential primitives that exposes: start(), lease(), refresh(), and close() helpers; built-in exponential-with-jitter retry hooks; and a lightweight health-check callback. 2) Adaptive retry presets - preconfigured profiles (interactive, background, batch) that tune backoff windows and idempotency rules. SDKs wire these into their stream managers so clients get better reconnect behavior out of the box. 3) Cross-SDK config normalization - introduce session_config schema with explicit fields (lease_ttl, refresh_margin, retry_profile) and mapping utilities in each SDK to keep platform behavior consistent. 4) Minimal public API renames: SessionManager -> SessionProxy alias (backwards compatible shim) and RetryConfig -> RetryProfile (alias + deprecation warnings). 5) Tests and examples: add end-to-end fixtures that run the same scripted session scenario against local mock server to assert parity of lease refresh timing and retry window alignment. 6) Docs and migration guide: update quickstart snippets and add migration note describing aliasing and recommended upgrade path. Why this is safe: migration shims maintain old names, and defaults are conservative. Breaking changes: none (no removal of existing symbols); we do however add deprecation warnings for the old RetryConfig name to encourage migration. Performance/behavior notes: adaptive retry presets reduce median reconnection time for streaming tears by ~18% in our perf lab for typical interactive profiles (see bench notes below). Checklist: - [x] Implement SessionProxy in Python - [x] Implement SessionProxy in TypeScript and Go with idiomatic signatures - [x] Add adaptive retry presets and mapping utilities - [x] Add unit + integration tests that exercise cross-sdk parity - [x] Update docs / quickstarts / migration guide - [x] Bump patch/minor versions in each SDK package files for release - [x] CI green and e2e smoke verified Bench notes: See perf/benchmarks/session-reconnect/README.md for lab numbers comparing legacy vs adaptive profiles. Migration notes: existing code using SessionManager or RetryConfig continue to work; consumers are encouraged to switch to SessionProxy.start() and to select a RetryProfile (interactive/batch/background) instead of hand-rolling backoff. Implementation details: the SessionProxy implements a small state machine with explicit states (idle, leased, refreshing, closed). Lease renewals are scheduled respecting refresh_margin and are cancellable on close(). RetryProfile maps to a {base_delay, max_delay, max_attempts, jitter_pct, idempotent_safe} tuple internally; streaming reconnection logic consults idempotent_safe to decide whether to resume or fallback to cold-start. Cross-language parity was validated by running the session scenario against a deterministic mock that simulates auth TTL jitter and transient network resets. Related: ENG-7421, PM-328, SUP-9142.

This PR introduces a small, language-idiomatic SessionProxy abstraction plus a set of adaptive retry presets that provide sane defaults for streaming and request/response flows. Performance/behavior notes: adaptive retry presets reduce median reconnection time for streaming tears by ~18% in our perf lab for typical interactive profiles (see bench notes below). Checklist: - [x] Implement SessionProxy in Python - [x] Implement SessionProxy in TypeScript and Go with idiomatic signatures - [x] Add adaptive retry presets and mapping utilities - [x] Add unit + integration tests that exercise cross-sdk parity - [x] Update docs / quickstarts / migration guide - [x] Bump patch/minor versions in each SDK package files for release - [x] CI green and e2e smoke verified Bench notes: See perf/benchmarks/session-reconnect/README.md for lab numbers comparing legacy vs adaptive profiles. Cross-language parity was validated by running the session scenario against a deterministic mock that simulates auth TTL jitter and transient network resets. Related: ENG-7421, PM-328, SUP-9142.

title: Docs/examples: add safe streaming retry guidance + sane timeout defaults
preview: Context: Support is seeing repeat tickets where customers use streaming without explicit timeouts and/or implement unsafe retries (e.g., retrying mid-stream without backoff, or treating disconnects as hard failures). This PR updates the Python SDK docs and examples to make the safe path obvious and copy/paste-friendly, aligned with the escalation/known-issues sprint. What changed: (1) Added a dedicated “Streaming: retries, timeouts, and reconnects” section with concrete patterns: retry only when the stream fails before completion; use exponential backoff with jitter; treat client timeouts separately from server disconnects; log/capture request IDs for Support. (2) Updated examples to show explicit timeout configuration (connect/read) and a recommended baseline for production. (3) Added links to the Known Issues article “Streaming timeouts and reconnect” so Support can reference a single canonical workaround page. (4) Added a small note on idempotency: if the caller may retry, they should provide stable inputs and handle duplicated partial output (client-side) rather than assuming the server will resume. Out of scope: changing SDK runtime behavior or default timeouts in code; this is docs/examples only. References: SUP-1842 (streaming resets, us-east) and SUP-1854 (truncated streams) and Linear ENG-4827. Validation: docs build + example lint; no runtime changes.
text: Context: Support is seeing repeat tickets where customers use streaming without explicit timeouts and/or implement unsafe retries (e.g., retrying mid-stream without backoff, or treating disconnects as hard failures). This PR updates the Python SDK docs and examples to make the safe path obvious and copy/paste-friendly, aligned with the escalation/known-issues sprint. Out of scope: changing SDK runtime behavior or default timeouts in code; this is docs/examples only. links: Confluence: Known Issues - Streaming timeouts and reconnect (workaround-streaming-timeouts-and-reconnect)

title: Fix SSE keepalive handling and ensure DONE terminates stream
preview: Context: Support escalations show Python streaming chat can hang when we receive SSE keepalive comment frames and the proxy coalesces lines. Our iterator ignored comment frames but also failed to detect the terminal [DONE] when it arrived without a trailing newline. Changes: (1) tolerate ':' keepalive comments, (2) robustly parse event boundaries, (3) treat [DONE] as terminal even if line ending is missing, (4) add a configurable stream_read_timeout with a clear error message. Includes regression tests using recorded payloads from SUP-4821.
text: Context: Support escalations show Python streaming chat can hang when we receive SSE keepalive comment frames and the proxy coalesces lines. Our iterator ignored comment frames but also failed to detect the terminal [DONE] when it arrived without a trailing newline. Changes: (1) tolerate ':' keepalive comments, (2) robustly parse event boundaries, (3) treat [DONE] as terminal even if line ending is missing, (4) add a configurable stream_read_timeout with a clear error message. Includes regression tests using recorded payloads from SUP-4821.

title: Standardize retry/backoff behavior + Retry-After handling (SDK parity v1)
preview: Summary: Implements the cross-language retry policy standard for the Python SDK: retry on 429 + transient 5xx + selected network/transport errors, exponential backoff with jitter, and proper Retry-After parsing (seconds and HTTP-date formats). Exposes configuration knobs on the client so customers can tune or disable behavior. Context: This is part of the SDK parity initiative (ENG-5120) to eliminate "works in TS but not in Python" inconsistencies, reduce support cases around intermittent 429/5xx/timeouts, and align SDK behavior with platform signals (Retry-After, request_id). Behavior (defaults): max_attempts=4 total (1 initial + up to 3 retries); retryable HTTP status codes: 429, 500, 502, 503, 504 (and 408 when the server responds with it); non-retryable: all other 4xx (e.g., 400/401/403/404/409/422) to avoid duplicate side-effects and billing confusion; retryable network errors: connect errors, DNS resolution errors, connection resets, TLS handshake errors, and read timeouts raised by the underlying HTTP client; backoff: exponential starting at 0.5s, capped at 8s, using jitter (full jitter by default) to reduce herd behavior; Retry-After: if present on a retryable response (notably 429), we honor it as the minimum delay, clamped to max_backoff to avoid extremely long sleeps in client code. Retry-After parsing details: supports integer seconds ("Retry-After: 5") and HTTP-date (RFC 7231 style). If the date is in the past or unparseable, we fall back to computed backoff. Config surface (new/standardized): ClientOptions: max_attempts (int), max_retries (int alias; deprecated warning if both set and disagree), initial_backoff (seconds), max_backoff (seconds), jitter ("full" | "none"), retry_on_status (list override), respect_retry_after (bool, default true). Request-level override: per-request options allow disabling retries (retries=0) for non-idempotent calls or workflows where the caller already handles retries. Notes on streaming: this PR does NOT add automatic retries for streaming responses. Streaming retries remain opt-in and should be implemented at the application layer with idempotency guidance (tracked separately); the retry middleware explicitly bypasses streaming iterators to avoid duplicating partial outputs. Implementation overview: Added a RetryPolicy abstraction used by both sync and async clients; implemented RetryAfterParser that accepts seconds or HTTP-date and returns a delay; integrated policy into the HTTP transport wrapper to centralize decision logic (status/network classification, sleep, attempt counting); ensured we propagate request_id into raised errors (when present) so callers can correlate retries with server logs; added unit tests using mocked responses and transport exceptions; updated docstrings and examples for configuration knobs and safe defaults. Files/areas changed (high level): redwood/_http/retry.py (new RetryPolicy + backoff), redwood/_http/transport.py (wiring), redwood/_client.py (options plumbed), redwood/errors.py (ensure request_id survives wrapping), tests/test_retry_policy.py, tests/test_retry_after.py, tests/test_transport_retries.py, docsstrings in client options and README snippets. Illustrative snippet (Python): options = RedwoodOptions(api_key=..., max_attempts=6, initial_backoff=0.25, max_backoff=10.0, respect_retry_after=True); client = Redwood(options=options) // client.chat.completions.create(...). Alternatively per-request: client.chat.completions.create(..., request_options={"max_attempts": 1}) to disable retries. Why these defaults: SRE guidance was to avoid retry storms under elevated 429s; honoring Retry-After and adding jitter is critical for protecting the gateway and improving tail latency. We intentionally do not retry 400-series validation errors to avoid duplicate actions and billing disputes (see SUP-29438). Testing: Unit tests cover: 429 with Retry-After seconds; 429 with Retry-After HTTP-date; malformed Retry-After; Retry-After in the past; 500/502/503/504 backoff increments and jitter; non-retryable 400 does not retry; transport exceptions (connect/reset/read timeout) retry; max_attempts respected; sleep is not invoked when retries disabled; streaming path bypasses retry wrapper. Rollout/compat: This changes default behavior for some callers who previously observed fewer retries (or immediate retries). Knobs are exposed; we also log a warning if legacy option names are used. Docs/migration notes will be updated in a separate docs PR for the cross-language parity release. Review notes: Ibrahim requested we clamp Retry-After to max_backoff (done) and ensure HTTP-date parsing uses timezone-aware values (done). Allison requested full-jitter by default and to document attempt counts (done). Marcus requested that we never log api_key values and that error messages mention configuration without printing secrets (no secrets logged; confirmed). Review conversation (highlights): Monica Patel: "Can we ensure Retry-After date parsing handles proxies that send RFC1123 with GMT?" Sabrina: "Yes, using email.utils.parsedate_to_datetime + UTC normalization; added tests." Ibrahim Khan: "Please avoid retrying 409s; TS had a bug retrying 400/409 and it caused duplicate side-effect concerns." Sabrina: "Confirmed: only 408/429 and 5xx per standard; added explicit test for 409." Allison Grant: "Default max_attempts=4 (3 retries) seems right; add jitter to reduce synchronization." Sabrina: "Implemented full-jitter; added note in docstring." CI: Initial run failed on lint (unused import in retry.py) and a flaky timing assertion in one test; fixed by mocking sleep and asserting call count/delays deterministically. Final checks: python-unit-tests (pass), lint (pass), typecheck (pass), packaging (pass). Merge: Squash merged into main; follow-up tickets: add conformance fixtures runner integration (ENG-5127) and update docs/migration notes (ENG-5128).
text: Summary: Implements the cross-language retry policy standard for the Python SDK: retry on 429 + transient 5xx + selected network/transport errors, exponential backoff with jitter, and proper Retry-After parsing (seconds and HTTP-date formats). Exposes configuration knobs on the client so customers can tune or disable behavior. Context: This is part of the SDK parity initiative (ENG-5120) to eliminate "works in TS but not in Python" inconsistencies, reduce support cases around intermittent 429/5xx/timeouts, and align SDK behavior with platform signals (Retry-After, request_id). Behavior (defaults): max_attempts=4 total (1 initial + up to 3 retries); retryable HTTP status codes: 429, 500, 502, 503, 504 (and 408 when the server responds with it); non-retryable: all other 4xx (e.g., 400/401/403/404/409/422) to avoid duplicate side-effects and billing confusion; retryable network errors: connect errors, DNS resolution errors, connection resets, TLS handshake errors, and read timeouts raised by the underlying HTTP client; backoff: exponential starting at 0.5s, capped at 8s, using jitter (full jitter by default) to reduce herd behavior; Retry-After: if present on a retryable response (notably 429), we honor it as the minimum delay, clamped to max_backoff to avoid extremely long sleeps in client code. Retry-After parsing details: supports integer seconds ("Retry-After: 5") and HTTP-date (RFC 7231 style). If the date is in the past or unparseable, we fall back to computed backoff. Config surface (new/standardized): ClientOptions: max_attempts (int), max_retries (int alias; deprecated warning if both set and disagree), initial_backoff (seconds), max_backoff (seconds), jitter ("full" | "none"), retry_on_status (list override), respect_retry_after (bool, default true). Request-level override: per-request options allow disabling retries (retries=0) for non-idempotent calls or workflows where the caller already handles retries. Notes on streaming: this PR does NOT add automatic retries for streaming responses. Streaming retries remain opt-in and should be implemented at the application layer with idempotency guidance (tracked separately); the retry middleware explicitly bypasses streaming iterators to avoid duplicating partial outputs. Implementation overview: Added a RetryPolicy abstraction used by both sync and async clients; implemented RetryAfterParser that accepts seconds or HTTP-date and returns a delay; integrated policy into the HTTP transport wrapper to centralize decision logic (status/network classification, sleep, attempt counting); ensured we propagate request_id into raised errors (when present) so callers can correlate retries with server logs; added unit tests using mocked responses and transport exceptions; updated docstrings and examples for configuration knobs and safe defaults. Files/areas changed (high level): redwood/_http/retry.py (new RetryPolicy + backoff), redwood/_http/transport.py (wiring), redwood/_client.py (options plumbed), redwood/errors.py (ensure request_id survives wrapping), tests/test_retry_policy.py, tests/test_retry_after.py, tests/test_transport_retries.py, docsstrings in client options and README snippets. Illustrative snippet (Python): options = RedwoodOptions(api_key=..., max_attempts=6, initial_backoff=0.25, max_backoff=10.0, respect_retry_after=True); client = Redwood(options=options) // client.chat.completions.create(...). Alternatively per-request: client.chat.completions.create(..., request_options={"max_attempts": 1}) to disable retries. Why these defaults: SRE guidance was to avoid retry storms under elevated 429s; honoring Retry-After and adding jitter is critical for protecting the gateway and improving tail latency. We intentionally do not retry 400-series validation errors to avoid duplicate actions and billing disputes (see SUP-29438). Testing: Unit tests cover: 429 with Retry-After seconds; 429 with Retry-After HTTP-date; malformed Retry-After; Retry-After in the past; 500/502/503/504 backoff increments and jitter; non-retryable 400 does not retry; transport exceptions (connect/reset/read timeout) retry; max_attempts respected; sleep is not invoked when retries disabled; streaming path bypasses retry wrapper. Rollout/compat: This changes default behavior for some callers who previously observed fewer retries (or immediate retries). Knobs are exposed; we also log a warning if legacy option names are used. Docs/migration notes will be updated in a separate docs PR for the cross-language parity release. Review notes: Ibrahim requested we clamp Retry-After to max_backoff (done) and ensure HTTP-date parsing uses timezone-aware values (done). Allison requested full-jitter by default and to document attempt counts (done). Marcus requested that we never log api_key values and that error messages mention configuration without printing secrets (no secrets logged; confirmed). Review conversation (highlights): Monica Patel: "Can we ensure Retry-After date parsing handles proxies that send RFC1123 with GMT?" Sabrina: "Yes, using email.utils.parsedate_to_datetime + UTC normalization; added tests." Ibrahim Khan: "Please avoid retrying 409s; TS had a bug retrying 400/409 and it caused duplicate side-effect concerns." Sabrina: "Confirmed: only 408/429 and 5xx per standard; added explicit test for 409." Allison Grant: "Default max_attempts=4 (3 retries) seems right; add jitter to reduce synchronization." Sabrina: "Implemented full-jitter; added note in docstring." CI: Initial run failed on lint (unused import in retry.py) and a flaky timing assertion in one test; fixed by mocking sleep and asserting call count/delays deterministically. Final checks: python-unit-tests (pass), lint (pass), typecheck (pass), packaging (pass). Merge: Squash merged into main; follow-up tickets: add conformance fixtures runner integration (ENG-5127) and update docs/migration notes (ENG-5128).

title: sdk: add helpers to query active optimization config version + pin version for deployments
preview:
## Summary
This adds first-class Python SDK helpers for the Safe Apply + Rollback project so operators and customer admins can (1) query which Optimization Config version is currently active for a given deployment/scope and (2) pin a deployment to a specific config version (with optional validation-only mode).

These helpers wrap the control plane APIs introduced in the platform work (Optimization Config Service) and standardize:
- request/response models (including scope fields)
- error handling for common authz/RBAC failures
- safe defaults (read-only methods require no additional permissions; pin requires explicit role)

## Motivation
Support and on-call workflows need a consistent way to answer "what optimization config is active right now?" and to execute a controlled rollback/pin without spelunking through raw endpoints. This is also needed for Private / Dedicated automation (Terraform/installer aside) where Python scripts are used to coordinate changes.

## API (new)
### Client helpers
- `client.optimization_configs.get_active_version(...)`
  - Returns the active config version for a given `deployment_id` and optional scope filters.
  - Intended for debugging, audit correlation, and post-change verification.

- `client.optimization_configs.pin_version(...)`
  - Pins a deployment to a specific `config_version_id`.
  - Supports `dry_run=True` to validate permissions and scope compatibility without applying.
  - Returns an operation payload (request id, effective scope, and the pinned version).

### Data models
- `OptimizationConfigScope` (tenant/model/region)
- `OptimizationConfigVersionSummary` (id, created_at, created_by, state)
- `OptimizationConfigPinResult` (deployment_id, pinned_version_id, effective_scope, request_id)

## Notes on permissions / RBAC
- `get_active_version` surfaces permission errors as `RedwoodPermissionError` with the server-provided `required_permission` when present.
- `pin_version` requires the new "optimization change operator" permission on the target scope. The SDK adds a short troubleshooting hint in the exception message (e.g., "Ask an org admin to grant optimization_config:apply").

## Implementation details
- Adds `OptimizationConfigsAPI` module under the client with clear docstrings and type hints.
- Uses existing request plumbing (no new HTTP stack).
- Both helpers accept `idempotency_key` and forward it to the API via `Idempotency-Key` header.
- Normalizes config version identifiers to string and validates non-empty.

## Example
```py
from redwood import Redwood

rw = Redwood(api_key=os.environ["REDWOOD_API_KEY"])

active = rw.optimization_configs.get_active_version(
    deployment_id="dep_7f8c...",
    scope={"model": "llama-3.1-70b", "region": "us-east-1"},
)
print(active.id)

# Pin (used for rollback/pinning during incident response)
rw.optimization_configs.pin_version(
    deployment_id="dep_7f8c...",
    config_version_id="ocv_20260122_0012",
    scope={"model": "llama-3.1-70b", "region": "us-east-1"},
    idempotency_key="rollback-2026-01-22-sev2",
)
```

## Testing
- Unit tests added for:
  - happy path parsing for `get_active_version`
  - pin request shape + idempotency header propagation
  - RBAC denied responses (403) -> `RedwoodPermissionError`
  - invalid inputs (empty deployment_id/version_id)
- Tests use `responses` to mock control plane endpoints.

## Backwards compatibility
No breaking changes. New methods only. No existing names changed.

## Release notes
- Python SDK: add Optimization Config version helpers (`get_active_version`, `pin_version`) for Safe Apply/Rollback workflows.

---
### PR checklist
- [x] Added unit tests
- [x] Added/updated docs and examples
- [x] Ran `make lint` and `make test`
- [x] Added changelog entry

### Screenshots / output
N/A (SDK-only change).
text:
## Summary
This adds first-class Python SDK helpers for the Safe Apply + Rollback project so operators and customer admins can (1) query which Optimization Config version is currently active for a given deployment/scope and (2) pin a deployment to a specific config version (with optional validation-only mode).

These helpers wrap the control plane APIs introduced in the platform work (Optimization Config Service) and standardize:
- request/response models (including scope fields)
- error handling for common authz/RBAC failures
- safe defaults (read-only methods require no additional permissions; pin requires explicit role)

## Motivation
Support and on-call workflows need a consistent way to answer "what optimization config is active right now?" and to execute a controlled rollback/pin without spelunking through raw endpoints. This is also needed for Private / Dedicated automation (Terraform/installer aside) where Python scripts are used to coordinate changes.

## API (new)
### Client helpers
- `client.optimization_configs.get_active_version(...)`
  - Returns the active config version for a given `deployment_id` and optional scope filters.
  - Intended for debugging, audit correlation, and post-change verification.

- `client.optimization_configs.pin_version(...)`
  - Pins a deployment to a specific `config_version_id`.
  - Supports `dry_run=True` to validate permissions and scope compatibility without applying.
  - Returns an operation payload (request id, effective scope, and the pinned version).

### Data models
- `OptimizationConfigScope` (tenant/model/region)
- `OptimizationConfigVersionSummary` (id, created_at, created_by, state)
- `OptimizationConfigPinResult` (deployment_id, pinned_version_id, effective_scope, request_id)

## Notes on permissions / RBAC
- `get_active_version` surfaces permission errors as `RedwoodPermissionError` with the server-provided `required_permission` when present.
- `pin_version` requires the new "optimization change operator" permission on the target scope. The SDK adds a short troubleshooting hint in the exception message (e.g., "Ask an org admin to grant optimization_config:apply").

## Implementation details
- Adds `OptimizationConfigsAPI` module under the client with clear docstrings and type hints.
- Uses existing request plumbing (no new HTTP stack).
- Both helpers accept `idempotency_key` and forward it to the API via `Idempotency-Key` header.
- Normalizes config version identifiers to string and validates non-empty.

## Example
```py
from redwood import Redwood

rw = Redwood(api_key=os.environ["REDWOOD_API_KEY"])

active = rw.optimization_configs.get_active_version(
    deployment_id="dep_7f8c...",
    scope={"model": "llama-3.1-70b", "region": "us-east-1"},
)
print(active.id)

# Pin (used for rollback/pinning during incident response)
rw.optimization_configs.pin_version(
    deployment_id="dep_7f8c...",
    config_version_id="ocv_20260122_0012",
    scope={"model": "llama-3.1-70b", "region": "us-east-1"},
    idempotency_key="rollback-2026-01-22-sev2",
)
```

## Testing
- Unit tests added for:
  - happy path parsing for `get_active_version`
  - pin request shape + idempotency header propagation
  - RBAC denied responses (403) -> `RedwoodPermissionError`
  - invalid inputs (empty deployment_id/version_id)
- Tests use `responses` to mock control plane endpoints.

## Backwards compatibility
No breaking changes. New methods only. No existing names changed.

## Release notes
- Python SDK: add Optimization Config version helpers (`get_active_version`, `pin_version`) for Safe Apply/Rollback workflows.

---
### PR checklist
- [x] Added unit tests
- [x] Added/updated docs and examples
- [x] Ran `make lint` and `make test`
- [x] Added changelog entry

### Screenshots / output
N/A (SDK-only change).

title: Add route experiment header support + troubleshooting logging
preview: ## Context Redwood Console route-level experiments (A/B/n) uses deterministic assignment based on a stickiness key and returns assignment metadata via response headers. Beta customers and Support reported confusion about how to pass the key and how to confirm which variant a request hit (see SUP-24877, INT-1193). This PR updates the Python SDK to (1) let callers provide a stable stickiness key in a standard way, (2) surface experiment/variant headers on responses, and (3) add opt-in troubleshooting logs that are safe by default. ## What changed - Add `experiment_sticky_key` (and alias `sticky_key`) optional argument to `Client.chat.completions.create(...)`, `Client.responses.create(...)`, and the generic request wrapper. The SDK maps this to request header `X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key`. - Parse and expose response headers: `X-Redwood-Experiment-Id`, `X-Redwood-Experiment-Variant`, `X-Redwood-Route-Id`, `X-Redwood-Experiment-Config-Version`. These are available via `response.experiment` and `response.headers` (raw) without changing existing payload models. - Add a minimal debug logger gated by `REDWOOD_SDK_DEBUG_EXPERIMENTS=1` (or `client_options.debug_experiments=True`). Logs include which stickiness key source was used (explicit arg vs derived), which headers were emitted, and whether the request used streaming/retry paths. By design, we do NOT log the full stickiness key by default; we log a short hash prefix for correlation and a "low entropy" warning when the key looks accidental (e.g., missing/empty/"unknown"). - Streaming: ensure assignment headers are captured on the initial HTTP response and persisted through stream iteration so callers can still inspect `assigned_variant` even if they only consume tokens. - Retries: make sure the retry wrapper preserves `X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key` and does not drop the request-id/experiment headers when constructing the retry request. - Docs/examples: update README snippets to show best practice (use a stable user_id / customer_id, not request_id). Add a short troubleshooting section: "Why do I see skew?" and "Where do I find the assigned variant?". ## Example ```py from redwood import Client client = Client(api_key=os.environ["REDWOOD_API_KEY"]) resp = client.chat.completions.create( model="redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize the following..."}], experiment_sticky_key=user.id, ) print(resp.experiment.experiment_id, resp.experiment.variant_id) # Always available: print(resp.headers.get("x-redwood-experiment-variant")) ``` ## Notes / design decisions - Header naming matches platform ADR/OTel conventions and the Console docs: we normalize access in Python (case-insensitive) but preserve the raw header map. - We intentionally avoid introducing a hard dependency on Console/experiment service types in the SDK; this remains a thin header feature. - Debug logging is opt-in and redaction-first (hash prefix only). ## Testing - Unit tests for header injection + parsing. - Integration-ish tests using `responses` to simulate streaming responses with headers. - Regression test for retry wrapper preserving experiment headers. ## Rollout - Safe to ship as patch/minor; no API removals. - Docs change should reduce Support churn around "missing variant headers" and "how to verify assignment". Related: ENG-9032 docs guide and the examples app update in redwood-examples.
text: ## Context Redwood Console route-level experiments (A/B/n) uses deterministic assignment based on a stickiness key and returns assignment metadata via response headers. Beta customers and Support reported confusion about how to pass the key and how to confirm which variant a request hit (see SUP-24877, INT-1193). This PR updates the Python SDK to (1) let callers provide a stable stickiness key in a standard way, (2) surface experiment/variant headers on responses, and (3) add opt-in troubleshooting logs that are safe by default. ## What changed - Add `experiment_sticky_key` (and alias `sticky_key`) optional argument to `Client.chat.completions.create(...)`, `Client.responses.create(...)`, and the generic request wrapper. The SDK maps this to request header `X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key`. - Parse and expose response headers: `X-Redwood-Experiment-Id`, `X-Redwood-Experiment-Variant`, `X-Redwood-Route-Id`, `X-Redwood-Experiment-Config-Version`. These are available via `response.experiment` and `response.headers` (raw) without changing existing payload models. - Add a minimal debug logger gated by `REDWOOD_SDK_DEBUG_EXPERIMENTS=1` (or `client_options.debug_experiments=True`). Logs include which stickiness key source was used (explicit arg vs derived), which headers were emitted, and whether the request used streaming/retry paths. By design, we do NOT log the full stickiness key by default; we log a short hash prefix for correlation and a "low entropy" warning when the key looks accidental (e.g., missing/empty/"unknown"). - Streaming: ensure assignment headers are captured on the initial HTTP response and persisted through stream iteration so callers can still inspect `assigned_variant` even if they only consume tokens. - Retries: make sure the retry wrapper preserves `X-Redwood-Experiment-Sticky-Key` and does not drop the request-id/experiment headers when constructing the retry request. - Docs/examples: update README snippets to show best practice (use a stable user_id / customer_id, not request_id). Add a short troubleshooting section: "Why do I see skew?" and "Where do I find the assigned variant?". ## Example ```py from redwood import Client client = Client(api_key=os.environ["REDWOOD_API_KEY"]) resp = client.chat.completions.create( model="redwood/llama-3.1-70b-instruct", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarize the following..."}], experiment_sticky_key=user.id, ) print(resp.experiment.experiment_id, resp.experiment.variant_id) # Always available: print(resp.headers.get("x-redwood-experiment-variant")) ``` ## Notes / design decisions - Header naming matches platform ADR/OTel conventions and the Console docs: we normalize access in Python (case-insensitive) but preserve the raw header map. - We intentionally avoid introducing a hard dependency on Console/experiment service types in the SDK; this remains a thin header feature. - Debug logging is opt-in and redaction-first (hash prefix only). ## Testing - Unit tests for header injection + parsing. - Integration-ish tests using `responses` to simulate streaming responses with headers. - Regression test for retry wrapper preserving experiment headers. ## Rollout - Safe to ship as patch/minor; no API removals. - Docs change should reduce Support churn around "missing variant headers" and "how to verify assignment". Related: ENG-9032 docs guide and the examples app update in redwood-examples.

title: sdk: raise ResidencyPolicyViolationError for data residency enforcement blocks
preview:
Context: Hosted data residency enforcement returns a deterministic error when a request would violate the org/project/API-key region policy (see ADR-022 + gateway work in ENG-4833). Today the Python SDK surfaces this as a generic APIError, which makes it hard for customers to catch and handle (e.g. prompt the admin to update allowed regions) and for support to quickly pull request traces.

What this PR does: Adds a dedicated exception type, ResidencyPolicyViolationError, and maps residency enforcement responses into it. The exception exposes (a) error_code (machine readable), (b) request_id (for support + audit correlation), and (c) help_url (docs link for remediation). The rest of the response payload is still preserved on the base error for debugging.

Behavior: - If the API returns HTTP 409 with error.code matching residency enforcement (e.g. RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION), raise ResidencyPolicyViolationError. - For all other errors, keep existing behavior (APIError subclasses like RateLimitError, AuthError, etc.). - For streaming: the exception is raised when the initial response is an error; mid-stream transport failures remain unchanged.

Why 409: Aligns with the public error contract for policy blocks (conflict with configured policy) and matches the gateway rollout.

Testing: Added unit tests for mapping and for preserving request_id/help_url. Also added a small integration-style fixture to ensure unknown codes do not get misclassified.

Docs: This PR does not update public docs; it only changes the SDK surface. A separate docs PR will reference the new exception in the Python examples.

Checklist: - [x] Linked to PM-1490 acceptance criteria (machine readable error + actionable guidance) - [x] Added tests - [x] No breaking change (new exception is a subclass of APIError) - [x] Backwards compatible parsing (help_url is optional; request_id may be absent on older gateways)
text:
Context: Hosted data residency enforcement returns a deterministic error when a request would violate the org/project/API-key region policy (see ADR-022 + gateway work in ENG-4833). Today the Python SDK surfaces this as a generic APIError, which makes it hard for customers to catch and handle (e.g. prompt the admin to update allowed regions) and for support to quickly pull request traces.

What this PR does: Adds a dedicated exception type, ResidencyPolicyViolationError, and maps residency enforcement responses into it. The exception exposes (a) error_code (machine readable), (b) request_id (for support + audit correlation), and (c) help_url (docs link for remediation). The rest of the response payload is still preserved on the base error for debugging.

Behavior: - If the API returns HTTP 409 with error.code matching residency enforcement (e.g. RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION), raise ResidencyPolicyViolationError. - For all other errors, keep existing behavior (APIError subclasses like RateLimitError, AuthError, etc.). - For streaming: the exception is raised when the initial response is an error; mid-stream transport failures remain unchanged.

Why 409: Aligns with the public error contract for policy blocks (conflict with configured policy) and matches the gateway rollout.

Testing: Added unit tests for mapping and for preserving request_id/help_url. Also added a small integration-style fixture to ensure unknown codes do not get misclassified.

Docs: This PR does not update public docs; it only changes the SDK surface. A separate docs PR will reference the new exception in the Python examples.

Checklist: - [x] Linked to PM-1490 acceptance criteria (machine readable error + actionable guidance) - [x] Added tests - [x] No breaking change (new exception is a subclass of APIError) - [x] Backwards compatible parsing (help_url is optional; request_id may be absent on older gateways)

title: Avoid stripping API keys; only remove trailing CR/LF
preview:
Context: SUP-4821. Some customers store the Redwood API key in secrets managers or copy/paste flows that include a trailing newline. The Python SDK previously used .strip() on the key, which can remove leading/trailing whitespace unexpectedly and cause auth failures when keys are not exactly preserved. Changes: normalize by rstrip("\r
") only; add a warning if whitespace is detected; add unit tests for newline and carriage-return cases; update docs string in README. Release: v0.9.4.
text:
Context: SUP-4821. Some customers store the Redwood API key in secrets managers or copy/paste flows that include a trailing newline. The Python SDK previously used .strip() on the key, which can remove leading/trailing whitespace unexpectedly and cause auth failures when keys are not exactly preserved. Changes: normalize by rstrip("\r
") only; add a warning if whitespace is detected; add unit tests for newline and carriage-return cases; update docs string in README. Release: v0.9.4.
